original_abstract: Thermally driven adsorption chillers (TDCs) and adsorption heat pumps (AHPs) offer a sustainable alternative to conventional vapor-compression systems by utilizing solar or waste heat rather than electricity. The performance of these systems is heavily reliant on the properties of the porous adsorbent, particularly its capacity for working fluids like water or methanol. While traditional materials such as silica gels and zeolites are widely used, they often suffer from limited uptake capacities or require high regeneration temperatures. Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have recently emerged as a highly versatile class of materials for these applications due to their exceptional porosities, tunable pore surfaces, and characteristic s-shaped adsorption isotherms. This focus article reviews the current state of MOFs in heat transformation processes, comparing their water and methanol adsorption performance to classical adsorbents. We discuss strategies for tailoring the hydrophilicity and stability of these frameworks through linker functionalization and examine the critical challenges of long-term hydrothermal stability over thousands of cycles. Furthermore, we address the importance of material shaping and the development of advanced coating techniques on heat exchangers to optimize heat and mass transfer, which are essential for increasing the power density of future adsorption-based thermal systems.of
humanized_abstract: Metal–organic frameworks in heat transformation: adsorption and regeneration behaviour focused on water and methanol adsorbents Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are one of the new classes of materials that have been recently explored extensively for heat transformation processes like adsorption chillers and heat pumps. The potential of MOFs for applications of thermally driven adsorption chillers (TDCs) and adsorption heat pumps (AHPs) has triggered increasing interest during the last few years. The application of MOFs is supported by the fact that their high porosity and adsorption capacity can be tailored by choosing different ligands. Thus, it is possible to prepare MOFs that show high adsorption capacities for working fluids (e.g. water and/or methanol) compared to classical adsorbents like silica gels or zeolites. Additionally, the s-shaped isotherms of MOFs can facilitate a lower and higher temperature adsorption, and a low regeneration enthalpy. This focus article gives an overview on MOFs of MOFs with respect to their applications in heat transformation processes. It presents the water and methanol adsorption performance of MOFs in relation to classical adsorbents. Furthermore, the influences of hydrophilicity through the functionalisation of MOF linkers and their long-term hydrothermal stability for at least thousands of thermal cycles are discussed. The article also focuses on the material shaping of adsorbents and coatings of heat exchangers that improve the heat and mass transfer. The latter has to be increased to improve the power density of future adsorption-based thermal systems.
